cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The passage is about Mr. Williams and his encounter with a policeman at a crossroads. First, the setup: the crossroads has a "SLOW" sign, so drivers should slow down but don't need to stop unless something is coming. Mr. Williams follows the sign by slowing down, looks both ways, and then drives across without stopping completely. He hears a police whistle and stops. The policeman accuses him of not stopping, but Mr. Williams points out the sign says "SLOW," not "STOP." The policeman then realizes he was in the wrong street. The question is asking what Mr. Williams was doing when he was stopped by the policeman. The options are A to D. Let's break them down: A) was driving at a high speed – But the sign said "SLOW," and Mr. Williams did slow down. The policeman didn't mention speed, just that he didn't stop. So probably not A. B) was driving onto the main road – The scenario says he was on a crossroad with a "SLOW" sign, implying he was driving onto the main road. He did slow down and then went across. So maybe B is correct here. The policeman's mistake might be about which street he's on, but the action of driving onto the main road is part of the context. C) was going to stop his car – Mr. Williams did stop after hearing the whistle, but the question is about when he was stopped. The action of stopping after being whistled at might not be what the question refers to. The key is what he was doing before the police action. The question is phrased as "when he was stopped by a policeman," so the action is happening as he was stopped. But the options might refer to what he was doing when the policeman stopped him. Since he was driving across the main road when the police stopped him, B might be better. D) was driving slowly – The sign said "SLOW," and he did slow down. The policeman's initial complaint was that he didn't stop, not about his speed. So D is likely correct because he was driving slowly when he approached the main road. Wait, the question is a bit ambiguous in timing. The exact moment when he was stopped by the policeman. The policeman caught him as he was driving across after slowing down. The text says "At once he heard a police whistle, so he pulled into the side of the road and stopped." So the action of being stopped (by the police) happened after he had already gone through the crossroad. However, the reason for the stop was that he didn't stop at the crossing. So the policeman noticed he didn't stop, so he blew the whistle to pull him over. So when the policeman stopped him, Mr. Williams was in the act of driving across the main road. The sign said "SLOW," he had slowed down, and then drove across. The key here is that the officer thought he should have stopped because of the sign, but it turned out the officer was in the wrong street. Looking back at the options: B) was driving onto the main road – This matches because he was driving onto the main road when he was stopped. The sign was at the crossroad leading to the main road, and he was in the process of entering the main road. D) was driving slowly – He did slow down when approaching the main road, so at the time of being stopped, he was driving slowly. However, the police whistle came after he had already driven across. So the timing is a bit tricky. Did he get stopped while he was still driving slowly, or after he had already gone through? The passage says: "He slowed down when he came to the main road, looked both ways to see that nothing was coming, and then drove across without stopping completely." Then immediately after, he heard the whistle and pulled over. So the action of driving across (onto the main road) was completed before the police approached. So when he was stopped by the policeman, he was in the process of driving across the main road (B), and at that time he was driving (presumably at a slow speed, so D is also a possibility). However, the question is phrased as "when he was stopped by a policeman," which is the moment the policeman approached him. But the police whistle came after he had driven across. So technically, he was stopped after he had driven across the main road. But the options don't mention that. The options are about what he was doing at the time of being stopped. If the police stopped him after he had driven across, then at that moment, he was on the main road and driving along it. But the question is about the action when he was stopped.
